coredata.dc:title: Multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe composed of Au nanocluster and carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent sensing Hg2+ and Cr6+
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Liying
Zhang, Lingyu
Ren, Guojuan
Li, Shuang
Zhu, Baoya
Chai, Fang
Qu, Fengyu
Wang, Chungang
Su, Zhongmin
coredata.dc:description: In this article, we reported a dual emissive multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe which was composed of gold nanocluster (BSA-Au NCs) and carbon dots (CDs) with a certain proportion, i.e. Au NCs-CDs. The dual emissive Au NCs-CDs showed pink fluorescence under UV light, and the dual emission peaks at 625 nm and 444 nm were corresponded to the existence of Hg2+ and Cr6+ respectively. The fluorescence color transformed from pink to blue when in presence of Hg2+. The blue fluorescence emission of CDs selectively quenched by Cr6+, whereas the red fluorescence of BSA-Au NCs was stable against Cr6+, which can be treated as internal reference. The difference response of the two emissions to Hg2+ and Cr6+ resulted in a distinguished fluorescent color change, which can be noticed by the naked eyes clearly. When the Hg2+ and Cr6+ were exposed to the Au NCs-CDs simultaneously, the dual-emission of the Au NCs-CDs were all reduced and exhibited the complete quenching of the pink emission. The detection limits for Hg2+ and Cr6+ were 1.85 nM and 5.34 nM, respectively. Significantly, it was successfully applied to determine the existence of Hg2+ and Cr6+ in real samples, which offered the advantages of simplicity, environmentally friendly and cost saving efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor-fluorescence
Au nanoclusters
Carbon dots
Detection
Mercury ion
Chromium (VI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detect of Hg2+ and Cr6+ using the multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe Au NCs-CDs
Practicability of multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe Au NCs-CDs
Characteristics of the BSA-Au NCs, CDs and Au NCs-CDs
Detection of Hg2+ and Cr6+ in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science Foundation for Post-doctoral Scientists of China
National Hi-Technology Research and Development Program (863 Program)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the NSF of China ( 21205024 ), the National Hi-Technology Research and Development Program (863 Program) (No. 2013AA032204 ), the project of Harbin Science and Technology bureau ( 2014RFQXJ151 ), the National Science Foundation for Post-doctoral Scientists of China ( 2012M520659 , 2013T60307 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of Au NCs-CDs and detection for metal ions by Au NCs-CDs.

(A) TEM image of Au NCs-CDs, the upper left corner of the picture (red circles) represented BSA-Au NCs, the lower right corner of the picture (blue circles) represented the CDs. (B) The ultraviolet absorption (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectrogram of the Au NCs-CDs. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) red-emission BSA-Au NCs, (b) pink-emission Au NCs-CDs and (c) blue-emission CDs, respectively. The inset showed the corresponding fluorescent photo images under UV lamp. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) Au NCs-CDs in the presence of (b) Hg2+, (c) Cr6+, (d) Hg2+ and Cr6+. The inset showed the corresponding fluorescent photograph under a UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The fluorescence emission spectra of (A) Au NCs-CDs and (D) the sole red-emission BSA-Au NCs in the presence of varying concentrations of Hg2+ (1 nM–80 μM). The linear relationship of fluorescence emission intensity ratio of (B) Au NCs-CDs and (E) BSA-Au NCs with different concentration of Hg2+ (λex = 365 nm). The contrast photograph of the (C) Au NCs-CDs and (F) BSA-Au NCs in the presence of varying concentrations of Hg2+ under UV light (λ = 365 nm).

The fluorescence emission spectra of (A) Au NCs-CDs and (D) the sole blue-emission CDs in the presence of varying concentrations of Cr6+ (1 nM–300 μM). The linear relationship of fluorescence emission intensity ratio of (B) Au NCs-CDs and (E) CDs with different concentrations of Cr6+ (λex = 365 nm). The contrast photograph of the (C) Au NCs-CDs and (F) CDs in the presence of various amounts of Cr6+ under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Au NCs-CDs solutions on addition of Hg2+ (80 μM) and other ions (100 μM). The inset showed an enlarged drawing of the peak at 625 nm. (b) Relative fluorescence intensity ratio of Au NCs-CDs aqueous solution in the presence of 100 μM of various metal ions (Cr6+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Pb2+) and Hg2+ (80 μM) (λex = 365 nm). The black rectangles represent the relative fluorescence intensity ratio at 444 nm and red rectangles represent the relative fluorescence intensity ratio at 625 nm. (c) Corresponding photograph under UV light. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Detect of Hg2+ and Cr6+ by using of Au NCs-CDs in real samples.

head.ce:title: Multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe composed of Au nanocluster and carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent sensing Hg2+ and Cr6+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Colleges of Heilongjiang Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin, Heilongjiang, 150025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, Jilin, 130024, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, novel, environmental and rapidly multicolorful fluorescent nanoprobe composed of Au nanocluster and carbon dots is reported, which can be used to intuitive detecting Hg2+ and Cr6+ separately and simultaneously. The detection limits were determined at 1.85 nM and 5.34 nM respectively. Notably, it can be applied to determinate Hg2+ and Cr6+ in tap water, lake water, sea water and human blood samples.

In this article, we reported a dual emissive multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe which was composed of gold nanocluster (BSA-Au NCs) and carbon dots (CDs) with a certain proportion, i.e. Au NCs-CDs. The dual emissive Au NCs-CDs showed pink fluorescence under UV light, and the dual emission peaks at 625 nm and 444 nm were corresponded to the existence of Hg2+ and Cr6+ respectively. The fluorescence color transformed from pink to blue when in presence of Hg2+. The blue fluorescence emission of CDs selectively quenched by Cr6+, whereas the red fluorescence of BSA-Au NCs was stable against Cr6+, which can be treated as internal reference. The difference response of the two emissions to Hg2+ and Cr6+ resulted in a distinguished fluorescent color change, which can be noticed by the naked eyes clearly. When the Hg2+ and Cr6+ were exposed to the Au NCs-CDs simultaneously, the dual-emission of the Au NCs-CDs were all reduced and exhibited the complete quenching of the pink emission. The detection limits for Hg2+ and Cr6+ were 1.85 nM and 5.34 nM, respectively. Significantly, it was successfully applied to determine the existence of Hg2+ and Cr6+ in real samples, which offered the advantages of simplicity, environmentally friendly and cost saving efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid and selective detection of heavy metal ions is important for both human health and the environment, since the heavy metal ions especially the mercury ion (Hg2+) and hexavalent chromium ion (Cr6+) could cause serious water pollution and potential threats to human health [1]. The heavy metal ions cannot be decomposed by biodegradation, and would get into the food chains, resulting in seriously harm. For instance, the Hg2+ can affect the function of human organs such as brain, heart, kidneys, liver and nervous system, even at a very low concentration [2–4]. And the noxious Cr6+ is extremely difficult to be chemically and biologically detoxified, hence, it can be easily accumulated in human nervous and cardiovascular systems when exposed to the contaminated water sources and air [5–7]. Therefore, the detection of Hg2+ and Cr6+ is indispensable for environmental monitoring.

During the past decade, series of methods have been developed to detect Hg2+ and Cr6+, including chromatography [8], stripping voltammetry [9], and atomic absorption spectrometry [10], etc. However, these techniques tend to be costly, technically complicated, time consuming and require expensive instruments. Besides, these conventional probes can help little on-site and rapid heavy metal ions screening from the view point of environment protection and pollution control. Recently, the fluorescence sensor is developed owing to their superior sensitivity, spatial and temporal resolution, quality and experimental convenience. Moreover, the fluorescence can be observed by the naked eyes to trace the target analyses [11–15]. Among the developed fluorescence probes, the carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of fluorescent nanomaterial with a size less than 10 nm, which have received much attention owing to their good water solubility, excellent photostability, lower toxicity and favorable biocompatibility [16–19]. Meanwhile, the conventional fluorescence probe, gold nanoclusters (Au NCs), are also attractive for heavy metal ions detection owing to their high sensitivity, ultrafine size, good photostability and low toxicity [20,21]. Ut supra, both the Au NCs and CDs present good optical properties, which make them be efficiently utilized for practical applications as fluorescent sensors.

Recently, the fluorescent detection probe based on carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) for detecting heavy metal ions including Hg2+ and Cr6+ have caught much attraction. For example, Zheng’s group explored a fluorescent C dot nano sensor for recognition of Cr6+ with the limit of 0.5 μg mL−1 [22]. Guo and co-workers invented a fluorescent probe of colorimetric detection of Hg2+ ions using protein-functionalized gold nanoparticles [23]. Ma and co-workers developed a bright C dots based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+, etc [24]. Tseng et al. reported of two-sized clusters including (Au7Ag and Au8) and (Au24Ag) for ratiometric sensing of Hg2+ [25]. Although the development of fluorescent probes have made great progress, there are still some malpractices in these probes for the detection of heavy metal ions, the most prominent question was that only one metal ion can be detected once. And, a single-colorful fluorescent probe only can display the change of fluorescence brightness by either “turn on” or “turn off” with analyses, which greatly limit their quantitative capability [26].

Therefore, it is a great significance to develop a multicolorful colorimetric fluorescent probe that satisfies the demands of rapidity and simplicity to operate, low cost, high sensitivity, good selectivity, and for different heavy metal ions sensing in complex environment at the same time. Great efforts should be put into designing and improving performance of multifunctional probe in a complex environment. Several groups have exploited the multicolorful fluorescent probes. Yan’s group synthesized a dual-emission fluorescent nanocomplex of gold nanoclusters and carbon quantum dots for sensitive determination of Hg2+ [27]. Du and Zhang’s group designed a kind of fluorescent colorimetry test papers for As(III) with limit of 5 ppb by using red quantum dots and cyan CDs [26]. Zhang et al. reported a fluorescent nanohybrid of gold nanoclusters and quantum dots for determination of Pb2+, which exhibited a continuous fluorescence color change from green to yellow in process of detection [28]. And Qu’s group designed a binary heterogeneous nanocomplexes constructed from gold clusters and carbon dots, which allowed sensitive detecting the highly reactive oxygen species in living cells [29]. These research works using binary emissive probe in detection with high contrast excelled the use of single emissive probe. However, the use of multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobes toward the wide color variations with different targets still remains a daunting challenge, because they lead to the formation of intermediate composite color, compressing the range of color variation [26].

Significantly, so far, a probe can only be used to detect one heavy metal ion, and it is imperative to develop a method for detecting multiple ions by a same probe. Inspired by this, we report the Au NCs-CDs multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe assembled by nanosized bovine serum albumin (BSA)-Au NCs and CDs, which can detect Hg2+ and Cr6+ individually and simultaneously. The obtained multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe possesses two emission peaks under a single wavelength excitation. When Hg2+ and Cr6+ existed in the sample simultaneously, the dual-emission was quenched simultaneously and the bright fluorescence color of the Au NCs-CDs were completely extinguished, which can be clearly observed by the naked eyes. Especially, as the concentration of Hg2+ increase, the emission intensity of BSA-Au NCs gradually decreased, leading to the clear fluorescence color varied from pink to blue. Contrarily, with the addition of the Cr6+, the emission intensity of CDs was shut off and the fluorescence color transformed from pink to red. The lowest detectable limits of Hg2+ and Cr6+ were 1.85 nM and 5.34 nM in aqueous solution, which were much lower than the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) standard for the maximum allowable level of inorganic mercury in drinking water is 2 ppb (10 nM) and for chromium in drinking water is 100 μg/L (0.34 μM). Moreover, due to the good photostability and biocompatibility of the Au NCs-CDs, the multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe can be further applied to detect Hg2+ and Cr6+ in real samples.

All chemicals and materials can be directly used without further purification. Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (HAuCl4) was obtained from Alfa Aesar (USA), bovine serum albumin (BSA) was obtained from Genview, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and acetone was purchased from the Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). The citric acid was purchased from Urchem (Shanghai, China) and diethylenetriamine (DETA) was purchased from Aladdin. The used metal salts Pb(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Cr(NO)3·9H2O, Cd(NO3)·4H2O, Co(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, Hg(NO3)2·1/2H2O, Mn(Ac)2, K2Cr2O7, Zn(Ac)2·2H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O. All glassware was thoroughly cleaned with freshly prepared 3: 1 HCl-HNO3 (aqua regia) and rinsed thoroughly with Mill-Q (18.2 MΩ cm−1 resistance) water prior to use. Mill-Q water was used to prepare all the solutions in this study.

The morphology and size of the Au NCs-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEOLFETEM-2100 transmission electron microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fluorescence spectra of Au NCs-CDs were recorded using a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer. The ultraviolet lamp used in the ultraviolet contrast diagram of the Au NCs-CDs is WFH-204b. The zeta potential distribution was measured by NanoBrook 90Plus PALS particle size and zeta potential analyzer (Brookhaven, USA).

The preparation of BSA-Au NCs was according to the reported literature [20]. In a typical experiment, HAuCl4 aqueous solution (5 mL, 10 mM, 37 °C) was mixed with a BSA solution (5 mL, 50 mg/mL, 37 °C) under rapidly stirring. Two minutes later, NaOH solution (0.5 mL, 1 M) was introduced, and the mixture was heating in water bath at 37 °C and stirring for 12 h. The color of the solution changed from light yellow to light brown, and then to deep brown. The obtained BSA-Au NCs was stored at 4 °C for future use.

The synthesis of CDs was based on the predecessors’ literature [22]. Typically, a 2.10 g (10 mM) solution of citric acid was mixed with the 3.5 g (35 mM) solution of diethylenetriamine (DETA) in a flask, and then the mixture was heated from room temperature to 170 °C in the oil bath and kept for 30 min. After the flask was taken out from the oil bath and naturally cooled to room temperature, the acetone was added into the reaction, and collected the precipitation by centrifugation. The solid was placed in a dialysis bag (cutoff Mn: 3.0 kDa) and dialyzed against water for 2 days to remove small molecules. Finally, CDs in the dialysis bag were freeze-dried to obtain the yellow solid. The original CDs solution used in this experiment was prepared by dissolving 0.0047 g of CDs solid powder into 4 mL of ultra-pure water and stored at 4 °C for future use.

The multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe Au NCs-CDs were composed of two fluorescent solutions (BSA-Au NCs and CDs) with a certain proportion, which was adjusted according to the multiply fluorescent color of the Au NCs-CDs. The influence of the ratio of the amount of BSA-Au NCs and CDs on the fluorescence color of the mixed solution was studied: initially, the as-prepared BSA-Au NCs solution was diluted 10 times, and the original solution of CDs was diluted 150 times, respectively. Thereafter, control the volume ratios of BSA-Au NCs to CDs were 1: 1, 1: 2, 1: 3, 1: 4 and 1: 5, respectively. The optimum proportion of the fluorescent color of Au NCs-CDs was 1: 3, which showed pink fluorescence under the UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detect of Hg2+ and Cr6+ using the multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe Au NCs-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Under the single excitation (λex = 365 nm), the Au NCs-CDs presented two emissions peak at 444 nm and 625 nm respectively, where the peak at 444 nm was attributed to the emission of CDs, and the emission at 625 nm was belong to BSA-Au NCs. The Au NCs-CDs showed pink emission under UV light (λ = 365 nm).

For the determination of Hg2+, different concentration of Hg2+ (1 nM–80 μM) was added into the multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe Au NCs-CDs, the mixed volume ratio of the Hg2+ and Au NCs-CDs was 1: 1. After mixed for one minute, the fluorescence emission of samples were measured, and the images of the samples under UV irradiation were collected (λ = 365 nm). The detection of Cr6+ was similar as the above operation method, and the concentration of Cr6+ was from 1 nM to 300 μM. In order to detect the specificity of the Au NCs-CDs to the Hg2+ and Cr6+, the other analogous heavy metal ions (including Cd2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe3+) were added to the Au NCs-CDs under the same condition (volume ratio was 1: 1). Each of the detection was repeated three times to ensure the stability of the experimental data. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practicability of multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe Au NCs-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The tap water, lake water, sea water and blood samples were utilized to estimate the feasibility and reliability of the proposed method. The sea water samples collected from the sea area of Bohai in China. The human blood was collected from the pathological laboratory, Harbin Normal University, Fang Chai. The human blood was diluted 500 times as a human blood sample for detecting ions. The tap water samples were obtained from the daily life without further filtering, the lake water and sea water samples used in the experiments were filtered to remove the impurities by a 0.22 μM membrane firstly. Then a series samples of Hg2+ (range of 1 nM–80 μM), Cr6+ (range of 1 nM–300 μM) and other ions (100 μM) were prepared by spiking real water with a standard solution. All experiments were according to the same process of the detection.
The detailed operation of cell culture and MTT assay were displayed in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the BSA-Au NCs, CDs and Au NCs-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The dual-emission Au NCs-CDs which composed of BSA-Au NCs and CDs can be treated as a multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe for detection of Hg2+ and Cr6+. The red-emission BSA-Au NCs and blue-emission CDs were selected as raw probes which were responded to Hg2+ and Cr6+ respectively. The architecture of the dual-emission Au NCs-CDs and the working principle for visual detection of Hg2+ and Cr6+ were illustrated in Scheme 1 . The BSA-Au NCs and CDs were synthesized according to the previous reports, respectively. Then, BSA-Au NCs and CDs were mixed together by a certain proportion to constitute a composition, the assembled Au NCs-CDs. The fluorescence spectra of the Au NCs-CDs exhibited dual-emission which was the composition of emission of the original red (BSA-Au NCs) and the blue (CDs). The mixture of red and blue probes in the volume ratio of 1: 3 leads to a pink fluorescence, and thus the maximal range of color variations is either “from pink to red” or “from pink to blue” (Scheme 1). Due to without significant interference to each other, the obtained Au NCs-CDs can be used to determine two kinds of heavy metal ions Hg2+ and Cr6+ synchronously by observing vivid visual colorimetric fluorescent signals. When the Hg2+ and Cr6+ presented in a sample simultaneously, the double emission peaks of the Au NCs-CDs were both reduced, meanwhile, the brightness of pink fluorescence was quenched completely. Most notably, the Hg2+ and Cr6+ can be distinguished by the different fluorescent color of Au NCs-CDs, when they were existed singly. Because the red fluorescence of BSA-Au NCs showed good specificity to Hg2+, while the blue fluorescence of CDs was relatively stable against Hg2+. Thus, the red emission was quenched by Hg2+, while the blue emission of probe could remained a bright blue background. Similarly, the CDs were responded to Cr6+ that could not disturb the red fluorescence of the BSA-Au NCs. So, the blue emission would disappear when exposed to Cr6+, while the ratiometric red fluorescence persisted and took on a bright red emission. The colorimetric fluorescent method possesses the advantages of simplicity and being cost-effective, and its signal can be directly monitor by the naked eyes. Overall, the designed multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe Au NCs-CDs could achieve the separately and synchronously detect of heavy metal ions Hg2+ and Cr6+.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of Au NCs-CDs illustrated the combination of BSA-Au NCs and CDs with good dispersion (Fig. 1 A). The component of BSA-Au NCs and CDs can also be recognized clearly, the mean diameter was estimated to be 2 nm, as shown in Fig. S1. The lattice spacing of 0.24 nm spacing was ascribed to the crystallite plane Au (111) and 0.21 nm was attributed to the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100facets) [22]. The analysis of TEM proved that the Au NCs-CDs were consisted of BSA-Au NCs and CDs with dispersed states, implying that both the BSA-Au NCs and CDs exhibited good stability after assembled, the Au NCs-CDs were prepared by simply mixing of the two probes. In order to investigate the interaction of the two components of the Au NCs-CDs, the zeta potentials of BSA-Au NCs, Au NCs-CDs and CDs were measured and supplied in Fig. S2. There is electrostatic repulsion between the BSA-Au NCs and CDs, but particles are not disturbed by other factors during the migration process, which was consistent with the particle distribution of the two substances observed in the TEM image.

Meanwhile, the optical behaviour of Au NCs-CDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 1B, the UV–vis spectra (Abs) of Au NCs-CDs showed apparent visible absorption peaks at 273 nm, which were corresponding to n–π* transition [30]. The maximum emission intensity (Em) of Au NCs-CDs was centrally located 444 nm and 625 nm with an excitation condition (Ex) of 365 nm. And as shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1, the decays are well fitted by double-exponential, revealing the average lifetimes of 13.52 ns for Au NCs-CDs. As an excellent sensor, the stability of fluorescence was studied due to it is a quite crucial parameter. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of the obtained Au NCs-CDs remained relatively stable after storage different time (0–7 d) at room temperate, which showed the Au NCs-CDs exhibited an excellent physical stable capability.

The fluorescence emission of Au NCs-CDs and its’ raw materials BSA-Au NCs and CDs were performed and compared. The fluorescence emission spectra showed that the BSA-Au NCs present a maximum emission at 625 nm, emitting strong red fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1Ca), and the maximum emission peak of CDs was at 444 nm with strong blue fluorescence (Fig. 1Cc). When the BSA-Au NCs and CDs were mixed with different volume ratios (1: 1, 1: 2, 1: 3, 1: 4 and 1: 5), the fluorescence emission intensity and color under UV light were investigated systematically. The optimum proportion of the fluorescent color of Au NCs-CDs was selected as 1: 3 (Fig. S5), which exhibited dual-emission bands at 625 and 444 nm under a single wavelength excitation (λex = 365 nm), and showed strongly pink fluorescence under the UV light (λ = 365 nm) (Fig. 1Cb). In order to expand the Au NCs-CDs to multi-detection, the fluorescence interaction of the original units of multifunctional nanoprobe should be discussed. And as depicted in Fig. S6, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs (BSA-Au NCs) and the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of the BSA-Au NCs (CDs) have no obvious spectral overlap region, thus there is no energy transfer between the blue-emission CDs and red-emission BSA-Au NCs. And because of that, there's no energy transfer between them, so the BSA-Au NCs and CDs can be treated as multifunctional-nanoprobe to detect Hg2+ and Cr6+ individually or simultaneously without significantly interfering with each other.

Due to the red emission of BSA-Au NCs in the Au NCs-CDs effectively quenched by Hg2+, thus, the fluorescent color of Au NCs-CDs was changed from pink to blue when exposed to Hg2+. Correspondingly, the fluorescent intensity at 625 nm decreased with the addition of Hg2+ (Fig. 1Db) compared to the blank of the Au NCs-CDs (Fig. 1Da). After exposed to Cr6+, the blue fluorescence of the CDs was quenched (Fig. S8c), and the luminous intensity at 444 nm decreased simultaneously (Fig. 1Dc), the fluorescent color of Au NCs-CDs was transformed from pink to red. When Hg2+ and Cr6+ coexisted in a same sample, the fluorescence quenched completely due to two kinds of original fluorescent probes BSA-Au NCs and CDs were responded at the same time, and the fluorescent intensity at 625 nm and 444 nm both extremely decreased (Fig. 1Dd), the brightness of fluorescent emission was disappeared, which certified the Au NCs-CDs can be used as a diversified colorimetric fluorescent probe for detecting Hg2+ and Cr6+ simultaneously. In addition, the multifunctional nanoprobe Au NCs-CDs could not only save time but also conserves resources due to the intuitionistic detection results.

To test the fluorescence responses for Hg2+, different concentration of Hg2+ solutions (1 nM–80 μM) were exposed to Au NCs-CDs respectively, and then the fluorescence spectra of all samples were recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the remarkable decrease of emission intensity of the Au NCs-CDs at 625 nm, with increasing concentrations of Hg2+ from 1 nM to 80 μM. Meanwhile, there was no significant change in the peak at 444 nm (with 1 nM to 10 μM of Hg2+), while when the concentration of Hg2+ increased from 30 μM to 80 μM, the peak of 444 nm produced an upward trend with a slight red shift (λex = 365 nm). This phenomenon may be caused by the response of the blue-emission CDs to the Hg2+ (Fig. S7c). The intensity of fluorescence of CDs can be affected by the high concentration of Hg2+, which could enhance the blue emission and strengthen the contrast effect of fluorescent color of detection. The relative ratio of fluorescence intensity of the dual emission Au NCs-CDs was chosen to evaluate the sensitivity. The relative fluorescence intensity ratio (I625/I444) of Au NCs-CDs decreased proportionately with addition of Hg2+ (Fig. 2B). A linear relationship can be established between I625/I444 versus the Hg2+ concentration. The liner relationship (range from 1 nM to 100 nM) by equation description was that I625/I444 = 0.03977–0.02505[Hg2+] (R2 = 0.999), where I represent the fluorescence intensity at 625 nm. Based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3, the minimum detection limit of Hg2+ was 1.85 nM. The slight change of the intensity ratios (I625/I444) lead to a distinguishable fluorescence color varied from pink to blue, which was available for visual detection Hg2+ by the naked eyes. As shown in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence color of Au NCs-CDs gradually converted from pink to blue, while the blue-emission CDs remained almost unchanged as backdrop. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity and color of the sole red-emission BSA-Au NCs became weak when exposed to Hg2+ (Fig. 2D and F), which was hardly to be distinguished by the naked eyes. And the BSA-Au NCs has a relatively poor linear relationship compared with the Au NCs-CDs (Fig. 2E). In comparison, the performance of detection of Au NCs-CDs was much more superior to BSA-Au NCs evidently.

In order to illustrate the specificity of the two original probes for two kinds of ions, and the mutual interference in the process of detecting two targets, detection for Hg2+ and Cr6+ by BSA-Au NCs and CDs were evaluated. The results were displayed in the supporting information. As can be seen from Fig. S7 and Fig. S8, the probe BSA-Au NCs was effective for detection of Hg2+ and exhibited inertia to Cr6+. Similarly, the CDs also exhibit the uniqueness for Cr6+ and relatively stable for Hg2+, thus, these results ensured the possibility of multi-detection. With the above mentioned condition, we evaluated the sensing capability of Au NCs-CDs for quantitative detection of Cr6+. The mixed volume ratio of Au NCs-CDs and Cr6+ was also 1: 1. The peak at 444 nm of Au NCs-CDs was composed of the blue light region of the BSA-Au NCs and the blue-emission peak of the CDs. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, with the increase of the concentration of Cr6+ from 1 nM to 300 μM, the fluorescent intensity of CDs at 444 nm gradually decreased, while the peak at 625 nm had no obvious change (λex = 365 nm). A good linear relationship of fluorescence intensity (I444/I0) versus Cr6+ concentration (range from 50 nM to 10 μM) was obtained, with the equation description was that I444/I0 = 0.94982–0.00532[Cr6+] (R2 = 0.996) (Fig. 3B). The minimum detection limit of Cr6+ was determined to be 5.34 nM. The fluorescence color of the Au NCs-CDs gradually turned from pink to red with the increase of the concentration of Cr6+ under the UV irradiation (λ = 365 nm), indicated the detection of Cr6+could be visualized by the naked eyes (Fig. 3C). Conversely, the fluorescence spectra of the blue-emission CDs can be quenched upon gradual addition of Cr6+ from 1 nM to 300 μM (Fig. 3D), but the single fluorescence quenching of the blue-emission CDs was hardly to be distinguished by the naked eyes compared with the Au NCs-CDs (Fig. 3F). The liner relations between the fluorescence intensity (I444/I0) of the CDs and different concentrations of Cr6+ were shown in Fig. 3E (I444/I0 = 0.97499–0.01771[Cr6+], R2 = 0.99). Accordingly, the single-color fluorescence probe CDs exhibited lower sensitive than Au NCs-CDs for the visual detection of Cr6+.

As a multifunctional nanoprobe, excellent selectivity is the most critical parameter. The fluorescence reaction of this method to detect Hg2+ and Cr6+ over other correlative heavy metal ions (including Cd2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe3+) were investigated at the same conditions, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The mixed volume ratio of Au NCs-CDs and various metal ions was 1: 1. There was inconspicuous response in the presence of 100 μM of other metal ions, while with Hg2+ (80 μM), the emission of Au NCs-CDs at 625 nm dramatically decreased, and produced an upward trend with a slight red shift at the emission of 444 nm. The fluorescence intensity at 444 nm substantial decreased with exposed to 100 μM of Cr6+, and no obvious transformation at emission of 625 nm. When the Hg2+ and Cr6+ existed simultaneously, the dual emissions exhibited a substantial decline. To illustrate the effects of Au NCs-CDs on Hg2+, Cr6+ and other ions more intuitively, a histogram of intensity ratio (I/I0) was designed for observation (Fig. 4b). The ratio of fluorescence intensity at 625 nm of the Au NCs-CDs was decreased about 75% by adding 80 μM Hg2+. The Cr6+ (100 μM) induced a great extent of decrease at emission of 444 nm, and the quenching efficiency was about 40%. By comparison, the fluorescence intensity ratio and fluorescence color have no obviously change in the presence of other metal ions (100 μM). Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescent color of Au NCs-CDs in the presence of Hg2+ changed from pink to blue under the UV light. And only the red fluorescence took on in the presence of Cr6+, which can be clearly distinguished. Compared with the results of adding Hg2+ and Cr6+, the phenomenon of other ions can be ignored both in the colorimetric results and intensity of the fluorescence emission. While the Hg2+ and Cr6+ existed simultaneously, the fluorescence was completely quenched. Thus, the above description indicated that the Au NCs-CDs can be used to separating or simultaneous detecting Hg2+ and Cr6+ with good sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and Cr6+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability of the Au NCs-CDs, the tap water, lake water, sea water and human blood samples were utilized to estimate the feasibility and reliability of the proposed method. The experimental results of the blank and real samples were demonstrated in Table 1 . The method of measurement and experimental phenomenon of sensitivity in tap water, lake water, sea water and human blood samples were followed the detection in ultra-pure water. Although the interference from chemical reagents and microbial existing in these samples, the Au NCs-CDs can highly selectively detect the practical Hg2+ and Cr6+ individually or simultaneously. The high sensitivity of Au NCs-CDs for detecting Hg2+ and Cr6+ in practical samples endow them to monitor Hg2+ and Cr6+ level in a continuous and real-time way (Fig. S9–Fig. S16). It is promising that the dual-emission Au NCs-CDs could be applied as a ratiometric probe for detecting Hg2+ and Cr6+ in real samples.

The results of MTT experiments demonstrated that the as-prepared Au NCs-CDs exhibited wonderful biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity (Fig. S17) [31]. When HeLa cells were cultured for 24 h that the cytoactive showed a small decreased. The cell viability was exceed 85% at the Au NCs-CDs concentration of 1.59–9.53 μg mL−1. Motivated by this, the Au NCs-CDs can also be used in cell imaging with excellent result (Fig. S18) [32].

The mechanism of detection by dual-emission multifunctional nanoprobe can be analyzed by investigating the two independent original probes, respectively. The quenching of CDs’ fluorescence upon addition of Cr6+ may be attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) [33]. A high efficiency of the IFE needs a good spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorber and the excitation band and/or emission band of the fluorophore [34]. And because the absorption bands of Cr6+ fully covered the emission and excitation bands of CDs. As a consequence, Cr6+ can not only shield the excitation light for CDs but also absorb the emission light from CDs. Therefore, the absorbance enhancement of Cr6+ could be successfully converted to fluorescence quenching of CDs [22]. The fluorescence quenching of Au NCs was due to the interaction of Hg2+ with Au+. For detection of Hg2+ by BSA-Au NCs dispersion forces between closed-shell metal atoms are highly specific and very strong [35,36], which are greatly magnified by relativistic effects, particularly when these interactions involve heavy ions such as Hg2+ (4f145d10) and Au+ (4f145d10) [37–39]. The sensing mechanism was based on the high-affinity metallophilic Hg2+-Au+ interactions, which effectively quenched the fluorescence of Au NCs. In summary, the combination synthesis of the multifunctional nanoprobe Au NCs-CDs was based on assembles the blue-emission CDs and red-emission BSA-Au NCs. There is no significantly mutual interference between the two raw probes, which ensured the related mechanisms effectiveness and impregnability.

In conclusion, we designed a smart multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe called Au NCs-CDs, which were composed of Au nanocluster (BSA-Au NCs) and carbon dots (CDs). Under the single excitation condition (λex = 365 nm), the Au NCs-CDs present two peaks at 625 nm and 444 nm respectively, which resulted in the pink-emission. The Au NCs-CDs can be used for colorimetric and fluorescent sensing different heavy metal ions Hg2+ and Cr6+ separately and simultaneously. This probe enables a simple and fast method for quantification detection of Hg2+ and Cr6+ with detection limit as low as 1.85 nM and 5.34 nM, respectively. Importantly, it can be further applied to determine the trace level Hg2+ and Cr6+ in tap water, lake water, sea water and blood samples. We believe that this kind of multifunctional nanoprobe strategies could be extended to the biological and chemical fields due to its great biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the NSF of China (21205024), the National Hi-Technology Research and Development Program (863 Program) (No. 2013AA032204), the project of Harbin Science and Technology bureau (2014RFQXJ151), the National Science Foundation for Post-doctoral Scientists of China (2012M520659, 2013T60307).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.01.192.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent detection of TNT and 4-nitrophenol by BSA Au nanoclusters
Tools and tactics for the optical detection of mercuric ion
Optical sensors based on nanostructured cage materials for the detection of toxic metal ions
Mercury concentrations in human placenta, umbilical cord, cord blood and amniotic fluid and their relations with body parameters of newborns
Selective visual detection of trace trinitrotoluene residues based on dual-color fluorescence of graphene oxide–nanocrystals hybrid probe
Analysis of Pb(II) ion sensing by crosslinked chitosan thin film using surface plasmon resonance spectroscopy
Societal benefits of reducing lead exposure
Determination of chromium in urine samples by complexation–supercritical fluid extraction and liquid or gas chromatography
Electrochemical determination of Ag-ions in environment waters and their action on plant embryos
Flame atomic absorption spectrometric determination of chromium (VI) by on-line preconcentration system using a PTFE packed column
A colorimetric sensor array for odour visualization
Controlling the color space response of colorimetric luminescent oxygen sensors
Design and color response of colorimetric multilumophore oxygen sensors
Ultrasensitive in situ visualization of active glucocerebrosidase molecules
Selective and facile assay of human immunodeficiency virus protease activity by a novel fluorogenic reaction
Fast one-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots by pyrolyzing ethanolamine
A novel one-pot route for large-scale preparation of highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots powders
Bioimaging based on fluorescent carbon dots
Multifunctional water-soluble luminescent carbon dots for imaging and Hg2+ sensing
Protein-directed synthesis of highly fluorescent gold nanoclusters
Horseradish peroxidase functionalized fluorescent gold nanoclusters for hydrogen peroxide sensing
On–off–on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium (VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
Colorimetric detection of mercury, lead and copper ions simultaneously using protein-functionalized gold nanoparticles
Complexes of glutathione with heavy metal ions as a new biochemical marker of aquatic environment pollution
One-pot synthesis of two-sized clusters for ratiometric sensing of Hg2+
Color-multiplexing-based fluorescent test paper: dosage-sensitive visualization of arsenic(III) with discernable scale as low as 5 ppb
Dual-emissive nanohybrid of carbon dots and gold nanoclusters for sensitive determination of mercuric ions
Fluorescent nanohybrid of gold nanoclusters and quantum dots for visual determination of lead ions
Heterogeneous assembled nanocomplexes for ratiometric detection of highly reactive oxygen species in vitro and in vivo
Highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots with broad visible absorption bands for visible light photocatalysts
A coumarin based Schiff base probe for selective fluorescence detection of Al3+ and its application in live cell imaging
Spectrochim. Acta Part A: Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Preparation of carbon dots for cellular imaging by molecular aggregation of cellulolytic enzyme lignin
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Green synthesis of carbon dots from prawn shells for highly selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Highly selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ based on fluorescence quenching of Au nanoclusters by Hg2+–Au+ interactions
Ultrasensitive sensing of Hg2+ and CH3Hg+ based on the fluorescence quenching of lysozyme type VI-Stabilized gold nanoclusters
Staples, Supramolecular chain assemblies formed by interaction of a π molecular acid complex of mercury with π-base trinuclear gold complexes

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li-Ying Yu was born in 1994 in Heilongjiang Province, P. R. China. She received her BS degree in chemistry in 2016 from Harbin Normal University and MS degree under the supervision of Professor Fang Chai in inorganic chemistry from Harbin Normal University. Her research interest is related to the preparation and applications of nanomaterials.

Dr. Ling-Yu Zhang received her BS degree in chemistry in 2009 and then received her PhD degree under the supervision of Prof. Chun-Gang Wang in 2015 from Northeast Normal University (NENU). She has studied as a co-cultured student in the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) from 2013 to 2015. She is currently working as a postdoctoral researcher at NENU. Her research interest is related to the preparation and relevant applications of multifunctional nanomaterials.

Guo-Juan Ren was born in 1992 in Heilongjiang Province, P. R. China. She received her BS degree in chemistry in 2015 from Harbin Normal University and MS degree under the supervision of Professor Fang Chai in inorganic chemistry from Harbin Normal University. Her research interest is related to the preparation and applications of nanomaterials.

Shuang Li was born in 1992 in Heilongjiang Province, P. R. China. She received her BS degree in chemistry in 2015 from Daqing Normal University and MS degree under the supervision of Professor Fang Chai in inorganic chemistry from Harbin Normal University. Her research interest is related to the preparation and applications of nanomaterials.

Bao-Ya Zhu was born in 1991 in the Nei Monggol Autonomous Region, P. R. China. She received her BS degree in chemistry in 2016 from National University of The Inner Mongol and MS degree under the supervision of Professor Fang Chai in inorganic chemistry from Harbin Normal University. Her research interest is related to the preparation and applications of nanomaterials.

Dr. Fang Chai received her PhD degree in physical chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Zhong-min Su from Northeast Normal University (NENU) (2010). Later, she worked as a postdoctoral researcher in the group of Prof. Chungang Wang at NENU. She currently works as a professor at Harbin Normal University. Her scientific interest is focused on the fabrication and applications of multifunctional nanomaterials.

Prof. Feng-Yu Qu received her PhD degree in inorganic chemistry from Jilin University (2006). Later, she currently works as a professor and doctoral supervisor at Harbin Normal University. Her current research is mainly focused on the fabrication and applications of multifunctional nanomaterials.

Prof. Chun-Gang Wang received his PhD degree in physical chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Zhan-fang Ma from Northeast Normal University (NENU) (2007). Later, he worked as a postdoctoral researcher in the group of Prof. Joseph Irudayaraj at Purdue University (USA). He currently works as a professor and doctoral supervisor at NENU. His current research is mainly focused on the fabrication and applications of multifunctional nanomaterials.

Prof. Zhong-Min Su received his BS (1983) and then his PhD in inorganic chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Rong-Shun Wang and Prof. Chi-Ming Che from Northeast Normal University (NENU) (1997). He has been a full professor at NENU since 1994. Later, he worked as a visiting scholar in the group of Prof. Chi-Ming Che and Prof. Guan-Hua Chen (The University of Hong Kong), in Prof. Koji Ohta’s group (National Industrial Technology Research Institute, Japan), and in Prof. N. Roesch’s group (Technical University of Munich, Germany). His research interests focus on functional material chemistry and quantum chemistry.
